scenario: A FEEL expression: =country in ["DE", "FR", "IT"]. Team wonders if in is membership test or range. FEEL in operator semantics?
question: FEEL in operator semantics?

options.text: The FEEL in operator is polymorphic: it tests whether a value satisfies ANY of the provided unary tests, which include ranges, specific values, and lists. For example, x in [1, 2, 3] returns true if x equals 1, 2, or 3. 10) tests range inclusion. x in (> 5, < 2) tests multiple conditions. The in operator supports all FEEL unary test types, not just ranges.
The for x in list return ... construct is FEEL's list comprehension / iteration expression and produces a new list of transformed values. b]. md:223-237.

FEEL has no membership or containment testing operator -- developers must use custom logic -- this approach has limitations. md:223-237). b] tests closed-interval inclusion. These expressions are used extensively in DMN decision table input cells and BPMN gateway conditions. Claiming the in operator has no such feature indicates a misunderstanding of core FEEL language capabilities.

md:223-237) evaluates a unary-tests expression against a value: the keyword in separates the value from the unary-tests. Right-hand side is a unary-tests expression -- not just a list. date("2021-05-31")] (closed date interval -- false); 5 in (3,5,7) (disjunctive -- true); 5 in [2,4,6,8] (list literal unary-test -- false). ) syntax for readable membership checks.

a.text: Incorrect. FEEL in operator: List membership: `feel 5 in [1, 5, 10] → true "DE" in ["DE", "FR"] → true "ES" in ["DE", "FR"] → false Range membership: feel 5 in [1..10] → true (closed) 5 in (1..10) → true (open) 10 in [1..10] → true (closed; includes endpoint) 10 in (1..10) → false (open; excludes) Compound unary tests: feel 5 in (7) → false (not 7) 5 in (<3, [5..10]) → true (in range) In DMN tables: Input entries are unary tests; in syntax is implicit (matches against input value). Cell value 5, 10, 15: equivalent to ? in (5, 10, 15) where ? is input. Compare with contains (string-only): contains("hello", "ell"): substring check. in: element / range membership. Compare with quantifiers (per Set 14 Q24): some x in list satisfies condition: existential. every x in list satisfies condition: universal. for x in list: list comprehension (different in usage in FEEL syntax). For the scenario (country in ["DE", "FR", "IT"]): Membership test. Returns true if country equals "DE" or "FR" or "IT". Common pattern for "is one of these allowed values." Negation: feel not(country in ["DE", "FR", "IT"]) True if country NOT in list. For complex collections: feel customer.id in (for c in customers return c.id) Check if customer ID is in a list of customer IDs. Performance: List membership: O(n) -- checks each element. Range: O(1) -- bounded comparison. For large lists: consider whether index-like structure better (DB lookup). For DMN inputs / outputs: Input entry with in: equivalent to listing values. Some implementations may not support full in syntax in input entries; verify. Common patterns: Pattern 1: Allowlist: feel user.role in ["admin", "manager", "supervisor"] Authorize specific roles. Pattern 2: Region check: feel customer.country in ["DE", "FR", "IT", "ES", "AT"] EU members for VAT logic. Pattern 3: Status check: feel order.status in ["pending", "in_review"] Active statuses. Pattern 4: Range check: feel age in [18..65] Working age. Anti-patterns: Overlong lists in in: should be data lookup. Repeated in` across many decisions: extract to BKM.
